title: Island's booming tourism comes with warnings against 'infinite growth'
author: Selina Bryan
fullText: Maria Island on Tasmania's east coast has stunning scenery, World Heritage-listed buildings and abundant wildlife.It has been a favoured destination for generations of Tasmanians and even interstate and overseas visitors make repeat trips.Elke Utner is often drawn to the Island when visiting Australia from Germany. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Elke Utner was holidaying in Australia from Germany and visited Maria Island for the fourth time."It's unique, I've never seen something like this anywhere else on the planet," she said."The landscape is so beautiful, the Painted Cliffs are wonderful." Ten years ago, around 21,000 people visited the island, compared with a record 53,000 in 2023. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)The island is only accessible to most people by ferry, and rangers' utes are usually the only vehicles.The island offers bunk-room accommodation and camping, but day trips have become increasingly popular.Tourist numbers have been steadily growing over the past 15 years.In the 2014-15 year, Parks and Wildlife Services reported 21,000 visitors. By 2018-19, the number had jumped 84 per cent to 38,700.The 2023-24 financial year saw a record 53,000 visitors to the island.Ms Bayly-Stark has noticed increased regulations at Maria Island. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Maddie Bayly-Stark grew up in Tasmania and still makes regular trips to Maria Island."It's nice, it's definitely more regulated than when I was a kid, but there's also more facilities, so it makes it easier to bring a young family out here," she said."There are more rules about bike riding and kicking balls and things like that, but I guess that's just what happens when you have more people in a space."Not all visitors follow the rules.People crowded around an echidna before a ranger asked them to step back. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Maria Island is a sanctuary for native wildlife and provides a home for some introduced species, including Cape Barren geese and Tasmanian devils.As visitors check in for the ferry, they are told to stay two to five metres away from wildlife — but social media is rife with videos of people showing themselves getting too close once they are on the island.Zoe Luo travelled to Australia from China and visited Maria Island for a day trip."It's really popular on Chinese social media, so that's why we know it and we planned here for a trip," she said.Zoe Lou (right) says she now understands the importance of protecting wildlife. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Ms Luo was part of a guided tour group that was asked by a park ranger to stop crowding around an echidna."The animal is really cute and people just want to take photos of it, but we need to protect the animal and we need to stand back," Ms Luo said."We are shocked by how you protect the land and also the environment here."Rosie Gibbs and Jas Potter want management to be more strict around wildlife protocols. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Jas Potter and Rosie Gibb, ecology students on exchange from the UK, said the island had "definitely been more populated than we would imagine".The pair watched a wombat quietly from a few metres' distance and photographed it, but were soon disturbed."A couple of other people came over, ran at it, got very very close into its space and it ran away and hid," Ms Potter said."There's a lot of tourists here and a lot of people who are clearly quite uneducated about how to treat wildlife properly," Ms Gibbs said."It's going in one ear and back out the other again, as has been showcased," Ms Potter said.Ms Potter said management should be more strict on the island "to ensure that people are properly educated".Critical lack of waterDry summer conditions on the island led to critically low water levels, prompting Parks and Wildlife Services to issue a safety alert in February, with water quality also being poor.Water levels are critically low on Maria Island, with authorities urging visitors to bring their own. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)It told visitors to bring all their drinking water.With no cafes or shops, people also need to bring their own food.Lani Payne, who visited from Western Australia, said she came prepared with "wraps for breakfast and lunch" and "heaps of water and snacks and all the goodies".The public is being warned of water shortages and poor water quality on the island. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Work is underway to cater to the growing demand, including a new vessel that will increase capacity by 20 to 30 passengers.The current ferry carries up to 165 passengers and is often fully booked in peak periods.A $9 million project is also underway to modernise the island's water and electrical infrastructure — including a new wastewater treatment plant and the decommissioning of the old one.Land clearing has brought fears of impact to the threatened swift parrot and forty-spotted pardalote.  (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Land-clearing under the project has drawn concerns that critical habitat for the threatened swift parrot and the forty-spotted pardalote has been lost. "We need to get our priorities straight here with Parks, are we here to protect this beautiful area for generations to come or to flog it off for infinite growth in the short term?" Greens MP Tabatha Badger said."We really need to see the Parks and Wildlife Service take this opportunity to re-centre and reconsider what their core job is and where the investment should be going."Our current trajectory is not sustainable."The public shared feedback on a draft plan to manage the island. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)Parks Minister Nick Duigan said the works would future-proof and protect Maria Island."These critical infrastructure upgrades on Maria Island are essential to supporting a sustainable visitor experience," Mr Duigan said.Island's management under reviewThe new Maria Island management plan is due to be released and implemented later this year. A report summarising the hundreds of responses received during the public consultation period found an overwhelming number of Tasmanians wanted the island to largely stay as it was.Among key points raised by the public were:Maria Island is "very different to many other destinations and should remain remote, basic and simple"That they "do not see a need nor do they agree with the recommended actions in the Maria Island Re-Discovered Visitor Experience Plan"The public was largely against increased access (helicopter, light plane, water taxi, cruise ships), on-island transport (motorised scooters, golf carts/buggies, mini-bus), food and beverage (shops, cafe, food deliveries) and accommodation (standing camp, luxury accommodation).Most of those who gave feedback were against development at the site. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)They wanted visitor numbers to be limited and a stronger ranger presence to provide visitor support, advice and services.German tourist Elke Utner agreed with the findings and said tourism had spoiled other experiences for her."A few years ago, we saw a cruise ship that was driving into Wineglass Bay and we didn't like it," she said."It's fine the way it is."The final management plan for Maria Island is due to be released and implemented later this year.
summary: Major upgrades are underway at Maria Island, but many visitors don't want the popular Tasmanian destination to change to accommodate rising tourist numbers.
